triadic_ontology.findings: Three recent papers advance triadic, relational ontologies. Christopher Isabelle’s “Triadic Relational Ontology” argues that after the primordial separation of “here/not-here,” a third irreducible relational field must be sustained for intelligibility to emerge, claiming dyadic language alone cannot reconstruct this origin. Damiano Migliorini’s “Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics” (July 2025) integrates analytic philosophy of religion with quantum-structural realism to model divine relationality as a transcendental feature of being, aiming to reconcile trinitarian dogma with contemporary ontology. Kenneth Hammat’s “A Triadic Neurophenomenology” (Oct 2025) proposes a three-domain model—object (material), idea (mental), and relation (axiological)—mediated by consciousness, offering testable neural signatures and AI implications.

ai_systems.findings: The autonomous-research landscape is converging on two complementary patterns.  Enoch, released to GitHub on 28 Apr, is the first open control-plane for long-running agentic work: it tracks GPU telemetry, gates dispatch with "wake windows," and packages AI-generated papers with full provenance so results can be audited rather than assumed.  Industry consensus has simultaneously narrowed to the orchestrator-plus-subagent architecture documented by FlowHunt—one supervisor LLM spawns short-lived workers that return only compressed summaries, cutting token cost 15× versus peer-to-peer designs.  Google’s Gemini 2.0 Ultra API, launched 4 May, now bakes transparent chain-of-thought into a 2 M-token context, eliminating prompt-engineering overhead for multimodal RAG pipelines.  Finally, the SII-GAIR lab’s ASI-Evolve framework (Mar 2026) closes the loop: four persistent agents (Researcher, Engineer, Analyzer, Cognition Base) evolve architectures across 1,773 autonomous rounds, beating human-tun [TRUNCATED]

local_news.findings: The Edmonds City Council will vote Tuesday, May 5 on Mayor Mike Rosen's nomination of Stephanie Lucash as the new city administrator. Lucash, currently deputy city manager of Kenmore, would receive a $226,068 base salary if confirmed. Separately, the council is poised to revive the volunteer Salary Commission to set pay for the mayor and council for 2027-2028 after the panel was suspended during the city's budget crisis.

Construction on the 88th Avenue West pedestrian improvements and overlay project resumes this week (May 4), weather permitting. The work will grind and resurface 88th from 194th Street SW, continuing through mid-May and marking the next phase of the multi-year downtown street upgrade program.

South County Fire commissioners will hold a public hearing Monday, May 5 at 7 p.m. to consider placing a $420 million fire-and-EMS construction bond on the November ballot. The funds would modernize stations, add medic units, and expand wildfire/EMS capacity across the district that now includes Edmonds.

Edmonds officials are bracing for a new statewide housing law (HB 2266) that takes effect June 11, forcing cities to allow transitional and supportive housing, plus indoor emergency shelters, in more zones. Mayor Rosen says the city needs time to study how the law interacts with existing development codes and neighborhood compatibility standards.

Edmonds Police Department reports it is on track to meet state-mandated de-escalation and mental-health training deadlines despite a statewide audit showing many agencies lag. Chief Loi Dawkins says EPD will finish the six required policy updates by June 30 to unlock $210,000 in new public-safety sales-tax revenue that began flowing in February.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has concluded 2025-26 auditions, but invites singers to email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the mailing list for August 2026-27 season auditions. Requirements: sight-reading, two-minute solo, and choral excerpts with quartet. Rehearsals: Tuesdays 6:30-9:30 pm at Epiphany Parish Seattle. Seattle Choral Company and Northwest Chorale have ongoing auditions—check their websites for schedules. Choral Arts NW presents Bach & Esmail: This Love Between Us on May 16, 7:30 pm at Trinity Lutheran Church Lynnwood; no audition required for this concert.
family.toddler: Edmonds Library (650 Main St) offers Toddler Storytime with Cami on Thursday, May 14 at 9:00 am and Baby Storytime with Kat on Tuesday, May 12 at 8:00 am. Lynnwood Library hosts Preschool Storytime on Wednesday, May 27 at 10:00 am. All events are free; check sno-isle.bibliocommons.com for registration and updates.

newyorker.title: On the High Line, Buddha Is the New Giant Pigeon
newyorker.dek: After the bird sculpture flew the Chelsea coop, the curator Cecilia Alemani oversaw the installation of a new work—a sacred sandstone colossus, based on one destroyed by the Taliban. Julian Lucas writes.
newyorker.text:
Before last year, Cecilia Alemani, the chief curator at the High Line, had never heard of—much less seen—a pigeon diaper. “We had an event for National Pigeon Appreciation Day,” she said, occasioned by a giant, hyperrealistic statue of the bird, called “Dinosaur.” Conceived by the Colombian artist Iván Argote, and unveiled in 2024, the sculpture spent a year and a half staring down commuters from its perch on the Spur, a section of the High Line that looms over the intersection of Thirtieth Street and Tenth Avenue. New Yorkers embraced it as a pop-up mascot; in Alemani’s words, “They recognized themselves in this idol hovering at the city’s edge.” More than five thousand attended the appreciation, donning pigeon drag, pummelling a pigeon piñata, and showing off their pigeon pets, diapers, “flight suits,” and all. “Dinosaur” was such a success that seven thousand petitioners tried to block its removal. “Everyone was very sad,” Alemani said. “But then they fall in love with the next one.”
She’d returned to the Spur to welcome the pigeon’s successor, a fifty-ton Buddha by the Vietnamese artist Tuan Andrew Nguyen. It was a crisp night in April. Drivers honked below, struggling to bypass an enormous crane and a flatbed truck idling by a turnoff to the Lincoln Tunnel. “On the way here, I wondered what all the traffic was about,” Alemani said. “Then I realized it was us!” The park had the air of a stage before showtime. Workers mixed mortar and drove a scissor lift to the edge of a concrete plinth surmounted by four steel rods—supports for the Buddha, whose sandstone body would slide down them in four parts, like beads in an abacus. As the first block was hoisted, Alemani darted to the railing to take a picture. “Look at those feet!” she said. “They’re so cute!”
Alemani, who was born in Milan, has the impish face of a putto in a Renaissance painting, framed by a mop of dark curls. She’s lived in Manhattan for twenty years, but still speaks of New York with a new arrival’s ebullience. “It’s already curated,” she said, gesturing grandly at the skyline. The waning crescent moon reminded her of a “giant banana,” prompting a staffer to mention Maurizio Cattelan: “All it needs is some duct tape.”
Few have taken a bigger bite out of the contemporary art world than Alemani. In 2022, she curated a landmark edition of the Venice Biennale, “The Milk of Dreams,” which juxtaposed Surrealist masterpieces with contemporary meditations on climate and the body. (It was the first to feature mostly women artists.) Last year, she was praised for her take on Site Santa Fe. But Alemani’s heart is with the High Line, where she relishes the challenge of curating for tourists, taxi-drivers, truckers, and sundry local skeptics. “They might be provoked, they might be challenged, they might be inspired, but there’s always an active relationship,” she said. “In museums, the encounter is more prescriptive.”
Alemani started at the High Line in 2011, but devised the “Plinth commission” years later, when the Spur was still threatened with demolition. “There was a pyramid of bottles full of yellow liquid, and chunky old keyboards that must have been thrown from the windows of the old post office,” she recalled. “So, of course, every artist I brought here fell in love.” Inspired by the piazzas of her native Italy, and the contemporary sculptures exhibited in London’s Trafalgar Square, she persuaded the park’s leadership to transform the space into a plein-air gallery.
The inaugural commission, in 2019, was Simone Leigh’s “Brick House,” a bronze bust of a Black woman with cowries dangling from her braids. Next was Sam Durant’s “Untitled (Drone),” a replica of the MQ-9 drones used by the military. Nguyen’s work, “The Light That Shines Through the Universe,” also reflects on warfare. An imagined re-creation of one of the two Bamiyan Buddhas—ancient cliffside sculptures in Afghanistan, which were destroyed by the Taliban—it augments the original with two brass hands cast from discarded artillery shells gathered across the country.
For now, the Buddha’s feet were stuck near the top of their support poles; a man in the scissor lift struggled to push them farther apart. Alemani was undaunted. “The city is going to wake up with a new sculpture that looks like it’s always been there,” she declared, which even the pigeon diehards might learn to love: “Maybe they can think of it as a reincarnation.” ♦

literary_pick.title: The Known World
literary_pick.author: Edward P. Jones
literary_pick.summary: Winner of the 2004 Pulitzer Prize and hailed by the New York Times as “the best book of fiction in the 21st century,” The Known World is a sweeping, morally intricate novel set in antebellum Virginia. It follows Henry Townsend, a formerly enslaved Black man who becomes the owner of his own plantation—and of enslaved people himself. After Henry’s death, his widow Caldonia struggles to maintain order as the plantation unravels: slaves escape, families betray one another, and the broader society teeters on the edge of chaos. Through a kaleidoscope of voices and perspectives, Jones explores the pa [TRUNCATED]
